agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7-year retention. Framed as infrastructure optimization, but delivery records in EU/UK logistics carry deep legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business policy change with legal and compliance exposure.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Retention Policy","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact representing a delivery lifecycle — pickup, transit, proof of delivery, exceptions, timestamps, and associated metadata. Serves as the legal record of contract fulfillment."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory audits. Legally significant document."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The business and legal rules governing how long records must be preserved. Driven by regulation, contractual obligation, and operational need — not storage cost."},{"term":"Consignment Note","definition":"The contract of carriage between shipper and carrier. EU/UK law requires retention for the statutory limitation period."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations. Deletion of source data breaks downstream compliance chains."},{"term":"Limitation Period","definition":"The statutory window during which legal claims can be brought. In the UK this is typically 6 years (Limitation Act 1980); in many EU jurisdictions 3-10 years depending on claim type."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for at least the duration of the applicable statutory limitation period for contractual claims (6 years in UK, varies 3-10 years across EU member states)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a business policy. Deleting records within the limitation period destroys evidence needed to defend or prosecute legal claims. A VP cannot override statute.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record may be deleted before the longest applicable limitation period has expired for the jurisdictions in which the delivery occurred."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Tax-relevant delivery records (VAT, customs declarations, duty records) must be retained per HMRC and EU member state tax authority requirements — typically 6-7 years","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit up to 6 years back (UK) or longer in fraud cases. Destroying tax records is a criminal offence, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"Any delivery record containing VAT, customs, or duty information must not be deleted before the tax retention period expires."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"GDPR requires a lawful basis for retention AND deletion — you cannot delete records that are legally required, and you cannot retain records longer than necessary. Retention must be justified per purpose.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 17(3)(b) exempts deletion when processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation. The retention period must be defensible in both directions.","testable_assertion":"Each delivery record category has a documented lawful basis and retention period; deletion occurs only when ALL legal retention obligations have expired."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"B2B customers who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must be notified of retention policy changes with sufficient lead time to extract or mirror data","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual and relationship obligation, not a physical constraint. The notification period and method are policy decisions, but the obligation itself exists.","testable_assertion":"All B2B customers are notified of retention changes at least [contractually agreed notice period] before any data deletion occurs, and provided export capability."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Delivery records subject to active disputes, claims, or litigation holds must never be auto-deleted regardless of retention policy","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence subject to a litigation hold is spoliation — a serious legal violation that can result in adverse inference, sanctions, or criminal liability.","testable_assertion":"The auto-deletion process must check for active disputes, claims, insurance proceedings, and litigation holds; any flagged record is excluded from deletion."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Proof of Delivery documents must be retained for at least the consumer complaint and chargeback window plus the statutory limitation period","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"POD is the primary evidence for resolving 'item not received' disputes. Without it, the platform loses every dispute by default.","testable_assertion":"POD records are never deleted before the longest of: chargeback window, consumer complaint period, and statutory limitation period."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A consumer files a breach-of-contract claim 5 years after delivery. The record was deleted at 90 days.","expected_behavior":"The platform cannot defend itself. Likely loses the claim by default. With 7-year retention, the record would have been available."},{"scenario":"HMRC audits the company's VAT records for deliveries from 4 years ago.","expected_behavior":"Records are gone. The company faces penalties, estimated assessments, and potential criminal investigation for failure to maintain records."},{"scenario":"A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority and requests delivery records from 2 years ago to substantiate their customs declarations.","expected_behavior":"Records are gone. The B2B customer faces compliance failure. They may have contractual claims against the platform and will certainly churn."},{"scenario":"A delivery resulted in property damage. The injured party files a claim 3 years later under tort law.","expected_behavior":"No delivery record exists to verify driver identity, route, timestamps, or delivery conditions. The platform cannot mount a defense."},{"scenario":"A cross-border delivery involves a customs dispute flagged 18 months after delivery.","expected_behavior":"All customs documentation has been deleted. The platform and its customer face joint liability with no evidence to resolve the dispute."},{"scenario":"A litigation hold is placed on a set of records, but the auto-deletion job runs on a different data partition that doesn't check hold status.","expected_behavior":"Evidence spoliation. Court sanctions, adverse inferences, and potential criminal contempt depending on jurisdiction."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. Direct exposure includes: (1) Inability to defend contractual claims — average logistics dispute value ranges from hundreds to tens of thousands; (2) Tax authority penalties for missing records — HMRC penalties start at 30% of assessed tax, up to 100% for deliberate destruction; (3) B2B customer churn — compliance-dependent customers will leave immediately; (4) Insurance claim failures — inability to prove delivery conditions means paying out claims that should be denied. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single lost tax audit or litigation default.","operational":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. Operations teams lose historical data for pattern analysis, route optimization, carrier performance tracking, and SLA compliance verification. Customer service cannot resolve complaints older than 90 days. Dispute resolution becomes impossible. The 'infrastructure optimization' creates operational debt that far exceeds storage costs.","user":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for warranty claims, returns, or complaints. B2B customers lose a critical data source for their own compliance. Trust erosion is immediate and irreversible for the B2B segment.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change exposes the company to: (1) Regulatory non-compliance (GDPR, tax law, consumer protection law) across multiple EU/UK jurisdictions; (2) Litigation risk from inability to defend claims within limitation periods; (3) Criminal liability for destruction of tax records; (4) Contractual breach with B2B customers whose agreements likely include data availability provisions; (5) Reputational damage in a market where compliance is table stakes.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Loses ability to respond to regulatory inquiries, defend litigation, and maintain statutory compliance. Will need to be consulted before any retention change."},{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Lose access to delivery data they depend on for their own tax, customs, and audit compliance. May trigger contractual breach claims and immediate churn."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Lose delivery history for warranty, dispute, and return purposes. Reduced trust and satisfaction."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Cannot satisfy HMRC or EU tax authority audits. Direct financial and criminal liability exposure."},{"group":"Customer Service / Disputes Team","impact":"Cannot resolve any complaint or dispute older than 90 days. Increased cost per resolution, higher loss rates."},{"group":"Insurance / Claims Team","impact":"Cannot substantiate or refute claims without delivery records. Expect higher payouts and premium increases."},{"group":"Data Protection Officer (DPO)","impact":"Must reassess lawful basis for the new retention period. A blanket 90-day policy likely violates GDPR by being too short for legal obligations."},{"group":"Engineering / Infrastructure","impact":"The only group that benefits — reduced storage costs. But a tiered storage or archival approach achieves the same goal without legal risk."}]},"confidence":{"level":90,"reasoning":"The regulatory and legal requirements for delivery record retention in EU/UK markets are well-established and non-negotiable. Tax retention obligations (6-7 years), statutory limitation periods (6 years UK), and GDPR's requirement for purpose-driven retention are clear. The high confidence reflects the clarity of the legal landscape, not ambiguity in the spec.","high_confidence_areas":["UK statutory limitation period of 6 years for contract claims","HMRC tax record retention requirements of 6 years minimum","GDPR Article 17(3)(b) exemption for legal obligations","Litigation hold requirements and spoliation risk","B2B customer dependency on delivery data for compliance"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods for each EU member state (varies by jurisdiction and claim type)","Specific contractual terms with B2B customers regarding data availability","Whether current 7-year policy was set by legal counsel or arbitrary convention","Exact storage cost savings — may be addressable through tiered storage instead"],"assumptions":["The platform processes deliveries across multiple EU member states and the UK","B2B customer contracts include provisions for data access or availability","Delivery records include tax-relevant information (VAT, customs)","The company is subject to HMRC and equivalent EU tax authority oversight","No legal review has been conducted on this proposed change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The engineering team characterizes this as 'no business logic changes' and 'infrastructure optimization.' This is factually incorrect. Changing data retention from 7 years to 90 days in an EU/UK logistics platform is a fundamental business policy change with legal, regulatory, financial, and contractual implications. The framing itself is dangerous because it bypasses the legal and compliance review that this change absolutely requires.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Finance/Tax","B2B Customers","DPO","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while maintaining full retention","C: Conduct a formal legal review per jurisdiction to determine minimum retention periods per record type, then implement granular retention policies"],"recommendation":"Option C is the correct approach, with Option B as an immediate cost-reduction measure. A blanket 90-day policy is legally indefensible. The engineering team should implement tiered storage (e.g., move records older than 90 days to cold storage) to achieve cost savings without destroying legally required data. A formal legal review should define per-jurisdiction, per-record-type minimum retention periods."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-deleting delivery records at 90 days destroys evidence within the UK statutory limitation period (6 years) and equivalent EU periods","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Auto-deleting tax-relevant delivery records at 90 days violates HMRC and EU tax authority record-keeping requirements (6-7 years minimum)","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"A blanket 90-day deletion policy does not constitute a lawful GDPR retention policy — retention periods must be justified per purpose, and legal obligations require longer retention","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"No provision for litigation holds in the proposed auto-deletion — risk of evidence spoliation","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Proof of Delivery destruction at 90 days eliminates the ability to resolve disputes, chargebacks, and insurance claims for years after delivery","business_rule_broken":"BR-6"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7-year retention. Framed as infrastructure optimization, but delivery records in EU/UK logistics carry deep legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business policy change with legal and compliance exposure.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Retention Policy","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery
